text: Robotic abdominal perineal resection, APR, is one of the latest minimally invasive techniques for removal of low rectal cancer. This technique is used when the tumor invades the anal sphincter complex, the oncologic APR, or when the patient's anal function will not tolerate a low anterior resection with coloanal anastomosis, a functional APR. These two different indications for APR also induce some variation in the perineal approach that we believe should be tailored. The robotic platform offers a stable vision and added precision for surgeons, making it possible to progress further the abdominal dissection in order to fulfill the required steps for each tailored perineal approach. There is a need for standardization of the procedure to make it reproducible and oncologically safe. We present our video showing the steps we believe should be taken as standard to perform robotic APR.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum through an umbilical port using a vertical incision, which is closed with a J-suture after placing four robotic ports in an oblique line. The ports are positioned starting from superior and medial to the right anterior superior iliac spine and ending in the left upper quadrant, spaced six to eight centimeters apart. An assistant port is placed parallel to the robotic ports line for instrument access. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg and right side tilt to expose the greater omentum under the liver and small bowel loops in the right upper quadrant, revealing the root of the small bowel mesentery, duodenal jejunal flexure, and inferior mesenteric vein. A swab is placed as a marker between the duodenal jejunal flexure and IMV, and a retraction stitch is used for peritoneal reflection exposure.

The robotic arms are docked with R-I holding a fenestrated bipolar grasper, R-II equipped with a camera, R-III using monopolar scissors, and R-IV holding a grasper for macro retraction. The assistant retracts the sigmoid colon laterally while R-IV retracts the sigmoid mesocolon to visualize the mesocolic pedicle. The sigmoid mesocolon is scored medial to the right ureter using monopolar diathermic scissors, defining the embryological planes between the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and hypogastric nerves. The IMA pedicle is dissected away from the nerves on the right pelvic sidewall and ligated one centimeter distal to the aorta. The dissection continues cranially to identify the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), scoring the mesocolon below it to find planes between the IMV and retroperitoneal fascia. The IMV is divided after secure ligation with locking clips, completing the mobilization with lateral to medial dissection of the left paracolic gutter.

The surgeon enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane and begins dissection behind the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The posterior plane guides lateral dissection, with nerves carefully dissected away from the mesorectum on each side. An anterior pretonal incision is made between the mesorectum and adjacent structures, such as the vagina in females and the prostate and seminal vesicles in males. The dissection continues caudally and laterally to connect with the previously dissected TME plane behind the mesorectum, progressing towards the pelvic floor. Tailored dissection of pelvic floor muscles is performed based on the type of abdominoperineal resection (APR) indication, with an extra-elevator approach for rectal cancer involving the anal sphincter complex. The anterior limit of dissection is at the seminal vesicles in males and the rectal vaginal septum in females, while the posterior limit reaches the tip of the coccyx and levator muscles.

The surgeon performs a functional abdominal perineal resection (APR) using an intersynthetic approach, extending the dissection through the pelvic floor inside the levator muscles to spare them. The intersynthetic plane is entered from above. The sigmoid colon is disconnected from the rectum using a linear stapler, and a colostomy is created using the distal end of the divided descending colon.

The patient is positioned in a prone jackknife position for perineal dissection. A longitudinal incision is made over the coccyx, extending to an elliptical incision that includes the anal verge. The incision is deepened from the skin to the coccyx and around the external anal sphincters. The coccyx is disconnected from the sacrum, and tailored excision of the pelvic floor muscles is performed based on tumor involvement. The specimen is removed perennially, and the pelvic floor defect is repaired using the greater omentum if possible, with drainage established through the abdominal cavity. A biological or covered composite mesh is used to reinforce the perineal defect repair, fixed like a hammock to avoid tension. The perineal wound is closed in layers, leaving the anterior part open with an iodo-povidone soaked dressing for drainage.
